title: I/O Architecture in Production AI Systems
key_insight: In production AI, I/O throughput — not compute — is usually what limits system performance.
body:
## I/O in Production AI Systems

### The I/O Stack for an LLM Inference Server

```
HTTP request (network I/O)
  → Request queue (memory I/O)
    → Tokenisation (CPU compute)
      → KV cache lookup (memory I/O — VRAM)
        → Attention + FFN (GPU compute)
          → Logit sampling (CPU compute)
            → Response streaming (network I/O)
```

Each step has its own I/O characteristics. The bottleneck shifts depending on:
- Request concurrency (network I/O bound at low batch sizes)
- Context length (KV cache I/O bound for long contexts)
- Model size (VRAM bandwidth bound for large models)

### Memory Bandwidth as the True Constraint

For a 70B parameter model at fp16:
- Parameters: 70B × 2 bytes = 140 GB
- H100 HBM bandwidth: 3.35 TB/s
- Time to read all weights: 140 GB / 3350 GB/s ≈ 42ms

At batch size 1, this means **inference is memory-bandwidth bound** — the GPU's compute units sit idle waiting for weights to arrive from VRAM. This is why:
- Quantisation (int4) reduces weight bytes → faster I/O
- Batching amortises weight reads across multiple requests
- Continuous batching (vLLM) keeps the GPU maximally utilised

### KV Cache: Trading Memory I/O for Compute

During generation, recomputing attention for all previous tokens every step is wasteful. The KV cache stores past key/value tensors in VRAM, turning O(n²) recomputation into O(n) memory reads per step — trading compute for memory I/O.

### Async I/O in Data Pipelines

```python
import torch
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# num_workers > 0 means separate processes handle disk I/O
# while GPU runs the previous batch
dataloader = DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=256,
    num_workers=8,     # 8 worker processes reading in parallel
    pin_memory=True,   # copies to page-locked memory for faster GPU transfer
    prefetch_factor=2  # loads 2 batches ahead
)
```

This pattern hides I/O latency behind compute — the goal of all high-performance I/O design.
common_mistakes: Ignoring data loading in profiling — torch.profiler often shows DataLoader as the bottleneck, not model forward pass
Using num_workers=0 (synchronous) in training — always use num_workers >= 2 for any real dataset
Storing large datasets in a single file — sharding into many files enables parallel reads
mini_exercise: Run torch.profiler on a training loop for 10 steps. Look at the timeline. How much time is GPU compute vs DataLoader? If DataLoader > 20%, increase num_workers.
